text: Welcome to this video of a laparoscopic hydroplasty combined with a fundoplication. We'll show you the basics of our technique.
Following the reduction of the hernia, the surgeon performs dorsal choreography, then places a mesh and proceeds with the fundoplication.
The surgeon identifies the hiatal hernia and proceeds to move towards the right crus of the diaphragm, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon dissects the dorsal aspects of the hiatal hernia, carefully separating the hernia tissue from surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissects the ventral aspects of the hernia defect, progressing towards the angle of His.
The surgeon dissects dense adhesions attached to the left crus of the diaphragm, preparing the area for further surgical steps.
The surgeon dissects the distal esophagus, revealing the size of the hiatal hernia as the surrounding tissue is carefully separated.
The surgeon returns to the right side of the surgical field to dissect and remove adhesions from the right cruz of the diaphragm, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
Of course we'll have to be careful here not to damage the vagal nerve.
The left crus of the diaphragm becomes visible as the surgeon continues dissection, revealing its anatomical position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon returns to the left side to remove the remaining adhesions at the angle of His, allowing for full mobilization of the distal oesophagus and complete reduction of the hernia.
We're now ready for a dorsal choreography.
A 32-gauge gastric tube is positioned within the surgical field, likely inserted into the stomach to assist with the procedure.
The surgeon places two to three stitches between the crura of the diaphragm, securing the anatomical structures in preparation for the fundoplication.
The surgeon uses non-absorbable sutures to secure the anatomical structures, with the left cruse of the diaphragm visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon places stitches, ensuring to capture sufficient tissue to prevent tearing of the muscle during the suturing process.
The surgeon places the second stitch approximately one centimeter dorsally from the first stitch, securing the tissue in the area of the diaphragm crura.
The surgeon has closed the gap between the crura of the diaphragm and prepares to introduce a surgical mesh into the operative field.
The surgeon introduces a synthetic bioabsorbable mesh, pre-configured for hiatal hernia repair, into the surgical field.
The surgeon introduces a synthetic bioabsorbable mesh through a twelve millimeter trocar and wraps it around the distal esophagus, preparing for hiatal hernia repair.
The mesh is positioned with an opening for the esophagus, and the ventral side remains visibly unclosed.
The surgeon tucks the mesh underneath the surrounding tissue, positioning it securely on the crown of the diaphragm.
The surgeon begins fixation of the synthetic bioabsorbable mesh around the distal esophagus, securing it in place for hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon attaches the right side of the synthetic bioabsorbable mesh to the ventral part of the right cruse of the diaphragm, securing it in place around the distal esophagus.
The surgeon wraps the mesh around the distal esophagus on the left side, ensuring it is positioned similarly to the right side.
The surgeon places a single stitch on each side to secure the mesh ventrally, ensuring it is fixed in position around the esophagus.
The surgeon places a final stitch dorsally to the right cruse to complete the fixation of the mesh, ensuring it is securely attached.
With the mesh securely fixed in place, the surgeon proceeds to perform the fundoplication, wrapping the upper part of the stomach around the lower esophagus.
The surgeon places a stay suture on the mobilized fundus, securing it in preparation for the fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon uses the stay suture to pull the fundus over to the right side, positioning it behind the distal esophagus.
The surgeon uses a gold finger instrument from the right side to grasp the stay suture, pulling it over to the right side behind the distal esophagus.
The surgeon successfully retrieves the opened nut of the stay suture and pulls it over to the right side, aiding in the positioning of the fundus behind the distal esophagus.
The surgeon prepares to perform a 270-degree fundoplication by attaching the fundus to the ventral lateral aspect of the distal esophagus.
The surgeon places the first of three stitches on the right side of the fundus, securing it in position for the fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon places the second stitch approximately one centimeter from the first stitch on the right side of the surgical field.
Non-resorbable suture material is visible as the surgeon places stitches during the Nissen fundoplication procedure.

The surgeon moves to the left side to perform the same stitching technique as previously done on the right side, preparing for the 270-degree fundoplication by attaching the fundus to the distal esophagus.
The surgeon places three single stitches approximately one centimeter apart on the surgical site, using non-resorbable material.
The surgeon ties the last knot ventrally and places an additional stitch posteriorly on the dorsal aspect of the right crus.
And this concludes this operation as well as this short video. Thank you for your attention.
